subject: Chemistry
title: Nanomedicine: Tiny Drug Carriers
description: A 60-second lesson on Nanomedicine: Tiny Drug Carriers.

hook.text: Nanoparticles can deliver drugs directly to cancer cells, sparing healthy tissue. The particles are smaller than a red blood cell.

buildup.text: Liposomes are hollow fat spheres 100 nm across. They encapsulate drugs, protecting them until they reach the target.

discovery.text: Tumors have leaky blood vessels with gaps that nanoparticles slip through. Healthy tissue has tight vessels that block them.

twist.text: Doxil was the first FDA-approved nanomedicine in 1995. It wraps toxic doxorubicin in liposomes to cut side effects.

climax.text: COVID mRNA vaccines use lipid nanoparticles to deliver mRNA into cells. The pandemic proved nanomedicine works at global scale.

punchline.text: Tiny fat spheres carrying medicine to precise targets. Drug delivery, revolutionized.

quiz.question: Why do nanoparticles accumulate in tumors but not healthy tissue?
options: Tumors have leaky blood vessels with gaps that nanoparticles can slip through
Nanoparticles are attracted to cancer cells by magnetic fields
Cancer cells actively absorb nanoparticles as food
